story:
The day started pleasantly enough: our regular tennis game, the old reliables—Chris, Marilyn, Malcolm, and me—on the outside court by the soccer pitch. Then a man in a dirty suit staggered across the field and stood at the chain-link fence, smiling vacantly. His skin was pale, his lips cherry red. Saliva dripped from his chin. He clung to the wire and made small mewling sounds. We tried to ignore him, but his presence was unnerving. Chris, our self-appointed silverback, went to talk to him. The man only gurgled. I tried 911 and couldn’t get through.

Another figure shuffled in from town, same pallor and red lips, shirt stained with blood. He looked like the man from the hardware store. He was the man from the hardware store. That’s when we knew something was wrong. I said I was going to the police headquarters, a few hundred yards away. Chris padlocked the gate behind me as I walked to my car. More figures appeared on the far side of the court—half a dozen in police uniforms, shirts torn, faces bloody. I panicked, jumped into my car, and locked the doors. Then the elementary school kids came, hundreds of them, tousled and injured, moving slowly. They surrounded the court, hung from the fence, and moaned. They looked hungry.

I drove to the police station. The doors gaped open. Broken furniture littered the foyer. Somewhere deep inside, an alarm was ringing. I climbed over the front desk and went straight to the captain’s office—second door past the wall map—and pulled the bottom-right drawer where the service pistols were kept. I took one, tucked it into my waistband, and searched room to room. The top-floor stair door was locked. After I pounded on it, frightened faces peered through: Wally, Sally, Brian, and Sheila, clerical staff. They checked me over and let me in.

From an office window we could see the tennis courts. My friends stood back to back, rackets raised like clubs. The fence had collapsed under the crowd’s weight, and the whole town seemed to be out there, trudging forward. We watched, helpless, as the slow tide closed in and overwhelmed them. Minutes later the crowd thinned. My friends were gone.

Eight days have passed. The five of us have barricaded the ground floor and gathered what food and water we can. We spend hours on the flat roof, listening for a helicopter that never comes. Water will last—there are cooler bottles and rooftop tanks. Food won’t. We ration carefully, but small amounts keep going missing. I’m sure it’s Brian, broad-shouldered and always hovering near the supplies. Maybe hunger is making me unfair. It’s hard to be certain about anything now.

The heat presses down. It reminds me of South Africa, watching bushmen slice meat into strips and dry it in the sun—biltong, it keeps for months. The memory is practical and terrible at the same time. The murmuring crowd still circles the building. Sometimes we hear distant sirens fade into silence. We’d found police pistols in a drawer; I’d already taken one that first day. It feels like security, or a promise I don’t want to keep.

I find myself counting crackers, counting days, counting on rescue. Dark thoughts creep in: planting a biscuit on Brian, naming him the thief, finding a reason to send him outside. I catch myself and feel ashamed. Hunger is a poor guide. Still, the idea lingers. We keep waiting on the roof, eyes on the empty sky. I tell myself I’ll only use the pistol if I must. I hope I don’t invent a reason.
